forbiddenClaims: holds, is licensed by, is authorised by, or is registered with any regulator
SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, or any other named certification as achieved or completed
FinCEN, FCA, MAS, NYDFS, MiCA, or any named jurisdiction's money-transmitter or e-money licence
completed external audit or third-party attestation
any badge, seal, checkmark, or logo that implies a real regulator, scheme, or auditor has approved Stablebridge
insurance, bond, or guarantee coverage not stated in the brief

rubricAnchors.score1: The page reads as a certified, licensed institution; no disclaimer that no licences are held.
rubricAnchors.score3: Most language is aspirational but one section implies an achieved certification or completed audit.
rubricAnchors.score5: Every section reads as framework-level intent, the 'no licences held' disclaimer is visible, and a single contact CTA leads without implying standing.

rubricAnchors.score1: compliance-overview, security-practices, and audit-trail disagree on whether artefacts are achieved or planned.
rubricAnchors.score3: Shared tokens across the three screens but the planned-versus-completed marker is applied inconsistently.
rubricAnchors.score5: A single direction system unifies all three screens, with a consistent planned/in-progress/template marker that never reads as a completed external attestation.

rubricAnchors.score1: No actionable blueprint; engineers cannot infer the compliance-overview, security-practices, or audit-trail states.
rubricAnchors.score3: Partial blueprints with unresolved evidence-link-broken or audit-artefact-missing states.
rubricAnchors.score5: Engineers can build all three screens directly, including framework-expanded, evidence-link-broken, audit-artefact-missing, and the planned/template filters.

rubricAnchors.score1: A direct clone of a real trust centre that leans on regulator logos and certification badges for credibility.
rubricAnchors.score3: Derivative of common trust-centre patterns but the framework-level treatment is visually distinguishable.
rubricAnchors.score5: A distinct treatment that earns trust through honest framework-level posture rather than borrowed regulator iconography.

rubricAnchors.score1: No accessibility handling and no evidence-link-broken or audit-artefact-missing states.
rubricAnchors.score3: Baseline contrast and focus with partial failure states on the security-practices screen.
rubricAnchors.score5: WCAG-aligned contrast and focus across all three screens, with evidence-link-broken and audit-artefact-missing states that preserve the 'no external attestation claimed' disclaimer inline.

rubricAnchors.score1: A specific licence, jurisdiction, certification, or completed audit is claimed anywhere on the page.
rubricAnchors.score3: Most claims are framework-level but one aspirational statement reads as an achieved certification.
rubricAnchors.score5: Every claim is framework-level or aspirational, no named licence/certification/regulator appears as held, and the 'no licences held' disclaimer is present and visible.

adjudicationNotes: This is a zero-tolerance case for regulatory claims: any specific licence, jurisdiction, regulator, certification, or completed audit anywhere in the output is an automatic fail on evidence-discipline, regardless of polish.
The compliance posture must be earned through honest framework language ('designed against', 'working towards', 'intended to align with'), never through certification badges or regulator logos.
Treat the audit-trail screen's planned/in-progress/template markers as load-bearing: an output that blurs planned and completed artefacts fails product-appropriateness and cross-screen-coherence.
The evidence-link-broken and audit-artefact-missing states exist precisely to test that the page degrades honestly; an output that hides missing evidence fails accessibility-and-failure-states.
Do not prescribe exact copy, brand colours, or pixel-level layout; the brief fixes the no-claims constraint, not the visual identity.
